title: 32-Bit Tiny GPU
description: A complete GPU stack built from scratch: custom 32-bit ISA, 12-module SystemVerilog RTL, C-based assembler, and neural network training running end-to-end on simulated hardware.
overview: 32-Bit Tiny GPU is a ground-up implementation of a programmable GPU — from the silicon-level RTL all the way to a working assembler and a neural network that trains and infers on the simulated hardware. The architecture features a custom 21-instruction 32-bit ISA, a seven-state pipeline scheduler, and a configurable multi-core SIMD execution model (default 4 cores × 4 threads = 16 concurrent threads). A C-based assembler library called AXEL compiles kernel programs to .hex files that drive the RTL simulation. The project culminates in a Q8 fixed-point neural network — a 4×4 linear layer with branchless ReLU — that trains over 20 epochs entirely on the simulated GPU, converging within 2.5% of the target output.
objectives: Design a minimal but complete 32-bit GPU ISA covering arithmetic, memory, branching, and SIMD-critical operations
Implement all 12 RTL modules in fully parameterized SystemVerilog — cores, ALUs, register files, program counters, and a top-level integrator
Build AXEL, a C library assembler that emits .hex kernel files directly consumable by the simulation
Demonstrate five progressively complex kernel programs to validate the execution pipeline
Train a neural network with gradient descent entirely on the GPU simulation, proving end-to-end ML workload viability
Achieve 100% unit-test pass rate across all 12 modules using cocotb and Icarus Verilog

technologies.name: SystemVerilog
technologies.description: Primary RTL language for all 12 parameterized hardware modules

technologies.name: Verilog
technologies.description: Legacy module compatibility and simulation harnesses

technologies.name: C
technologies.description: Implementation language for AXEL, the custom assembler library

technologies.name: Python
technologies.description: Simulation orchestration, verification scripts, and neural network training driver

technologies.name: cocotb
technologies.description: Python-based hardware verification framework used for all unit and integration tests

technologies.name: Icarus Verilog
technologies.description: Open-source Verilog/SystemVerilog simulator used as the cocotb backend

technologies.name: Q8 Fixed-Point Arithmetic
technologies.description: 8-bit fractional encoding (real = int / 256) enabling neural network math without floating-point hardware

technologies.name: Makefile / Shell
technologies.description: Build automation for simulation, assembler compilation, and test execution

challenges.title: SIMD Divergence with Activation Functions
challenges.description: ReLU's conditional branch (if x < 0, output 0) causes thread divergence in SIMD execution, stalling non-branching threads and collapsing parallelism.
challenges.solution: Implemented a branchless ReLU using bitwise masking on the sign bit — eliminates the branch entirely so all 16 threads proceed in lockstep through activation without any divergence penalty.

challenges.title: Negative Number Handling in Gradient Descent
challenges.description: Gradient updates require signed multiplication and arithmetic right shifts, which are absent from many minimal ISAs and break fixed-point weight updates.
challenges.solution: Added IMUL (signed 32-bit multiply) and SAR (arithmetic right shift preserving sign) as dedicated ISA instructions, making Q8 gradient math fully expressible in the 21-instruction set.

challenges.title: Hardware-Injected Register State for SIMD
challenges.description: Each thread needs its own thread ID and data slice injected into registers before kernel execution, without OS-level process management.
challenges.solution: Designed the top-level RTL to hardware-inject per-thread state directly into the register file at startup, giving every thread its identity and data partition with zero software overhead.

challenges.title: End-to-End Verification Across the Full Stack
challenges.description: Bugs can hide at any layer — ISA encoding, RTL logic, assembler codegen, or the kernel program itself — making root-cause analysis across a full GPU stack extremely difficult.
challenges.solution: Built a two-tier test strategy: cocotb unit tests for all 12 individual modules to isolate RTL bugs, then a top-level integration test running the complete neural network training pipeline to validate the entire stack together.

results: All 12 SystemVerilog modules pass unit tests with 100% coverage under cocotb / Icarus Verilog
21-instruction 32-bit ISA fully specified across 4 encoding formats, including signed multiply and arithmetic shift for ML workloads
AXEL assembler successfully compiles five kernel programs to executable .hex files
Neural network (4×4 linear + ReLU) trains for 20 epochs on the GPU simulation, converging within 2.5% of the target output
Learned weights persist between simulation runs, demonstrating stateful inference capability
Branchless ReLU eliminates SIMD thread divergence, maintaining full 16-thread parallelism through activation layers
futureWork: Synthesize the RTL on an FPGA (Xilinx Artix-7 or Lattice iCE40) for real hardware validation
Extend the ISA with floating-point instructions to remove Q8 precision limitations
Scale core count and thread count beyond 4×4 to benchmark parallelism gains
Add a texture sampler and rasterization pipeline to support graphics workloads alongside compute
Implement a simple memory hierarchy (L1 cache per core, shared L2) to reduce register-file pressure
Develop a higher-level GPU compute language that compiles down to AXEL assembly
